title: Interactive flip pages
subtitle: Building hinged panels, fold-out pockets, and accordion inserts that open and close within a scrapbook page
excerpt: An interactive element in a scrapbook page invites engagement beyond looking. A flip-up panel hides a journalling block. A pocket holds a folded letter. An accordion insert unfolds to show a sequence of small photos. Each of these is made with the same basic tools, a bone folder, a craft knife, and a ruler, and takes ten to twenty minutes to construct before being attached to the page.
sourceNotes: General scrapbooking and bookarts tradition; interactive page elements draw on bookbinding and paper engineering techniques widely documented in craft literature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hinged paper element adhered to a scrapbook page along one edge, which lifts open to reveal content beneath or on its reverse face. The hinge is a scored fold line 5-8 mm from the attachment edge; the panel should fold open completely flat without resistance.

A strip of paper with alternating valley and mountain folds producing a zigzag structure. Collapses flat for storage within a page, extends to show a sequence of images or text. The standard fold for photo strips and mini-timelines within a scrapbook.

Text written on the reverse face of a flip panel, or inside a pocket, visible only when the element is opened. Hidden journalling allows personal or detailed notes to accompany a spread without competing visually with the photos.

content.text: is the simplest interactive element and the best starting point. It is just a rectangle of paper hinged along one edge to the page surface, a single scored fold line and a thin strip of adhesive are all that hold it in place. The panel can carry a photo on its face and
Cut the panel from 130 gsm cartridge: it should be the same size as (or smaller than) the photo it will display. Add 8 mm to one edge for the hinge strip.
Score the hinge line 8 mm from the hinge edge. Fold the hinge strip back and run the bone folder firmly along the fold. The panel should lie completely flat when open and closed.

Apply a thin line of acid-free PVA to the hinge strip only. Press the hinge strip onto the page at the planned position and hold for 60 seconds. The panel should now open and close freely without the hinge strip lifting.

Cut a strip of 90 gsm paper at the height of the page, 5-6 times the width of the intended panel space. Score fold lines at regular intervals (equal to the final panel width when collapsed) alternating as valley and mountain folds. Fold along all scores with the bone folder. The result is an
that collapses flat and stores inside a page pocket or behind a flip panel.

items.symptom: The flip panel does not lie flat when open, it springs back
items.cause: The hinge was not scored before folding, so the paper is fighting the bend.
items.fix: Score the hinge line firmly with the scoring tool against a metal ruler before any folding. On papers with a coating, score both faces of the hinge for a clean open lie.

items.symptom: The accordion insert does not compress fully flat
items.cause: Fold intervals are slightly unequal, causing the collapsed accordion to be thicker at one end.
items.fix: Measure and mark all fold lines before scoring. Use a set square to check that fold lines are parallel before scoring, particularly on a long accordion strip.
